---
title: "IE 360 — Homework 2: ARIMA Modeling and Forecast Comparison"
author: "İdil Gündüz | Student ID: 2022402195"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    theme: cosmo
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-important}
## Submission Requirements

- **Deadline:** May 1, 2026, 23:59
- **Weight:** 7.5% of course grade
- **Format:** Render this `.qmd` to HTML and commit **both** files to your GitHub repo under `hw2/`
- **Folder structure:** `hw2/HW2_ARIMA_ModelComparison.qmd`, `hw2/HW2_ARIMA_ModelComparison.html`, `hw2/data/`
- **Commits:** You must have multiple meaningful commits spread across your working period. Single-commit or same-day-only submissions will be flagged and penalized.
- **AI Disclosure:** You **must** include the AI Usage Disclosure section at the end of this document (even if you did not use AI tools). Missing disclosure = penalty.
:::

```{python setup}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

STUDENT_ID  = 2022402195
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % (2**31))

offset_days = STUDENT_ID % 90
test_start  = pd.Timestamp("2025-07-01") + pd.Timedelta(days=offset_days)
test_end    = test_start + pd.Timedelta(days=27)
print(f"Test window: {test_start.date()} to {test_end.date()}")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## About This Assignment

In this homework you will apply ARIMA modeling to Turkey's hourly electricity prices (MCP/PTF) and compare it against a regression baseline and a naive benchmark using a rolling 1-day-ahead forecast. This is the same target variable as the class project --- methods you develop here will directly transfer to the forecasting competition starting May 4.

**Your personalized test window** is printed above. All your evaluation metrics will be computed over this window. Every student has a different window, so your results will differ from your classmates.
:::

# Data Acquisition and Exploration (15 pts)

## Load MCP/PTF Data (5 pts)

Download hourly MCP/PTF (Market Clearing Price) data from EPIAS for **2022-01-01 to 2026-03-31**. You can use `eptr2` (Python) or manual download from the EPIAS Transparency Platform, then save as CSV.

Split the data using your personalized test window printed above:

- **Training set:** 2022-01-01 to the day before your `test_start`
- **Test set:** Your 4-week window (`test_start` to `test_end`)

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# --- Load merged hourly MCP/PTF data --------------------------------------
df = pd.read_csv('data/mcp_hourly.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
df = df.rename(columns={'date': 'Date', 'mcp': 'Price'})
df = df.sort_values('Date').reset_index(drop=True)

# --- Train / test split (hour-based, including last 23 hours of test_end) -
train_dt = df[df['Date'] <  test_start].copy()
test_dt  = df[(df['Date'] >= test_start) &
              (df['Date'] <= test_end + pd.Timedelta(hours=23))].copy()

print(f"Training observations : {len(train_dt):,}")
print(f"Test observations     : {len(test_dt):,}")
print(f"Date range            : {df['Date'].min()}  →  {df['Date'].max()}")

print("\nSummary statistics (Price, TL/MWh):")
print(df['Price'].describe()[['min', 'max', 'mean', '50%']]
      .rename({'50%': 'median'}).round(2))
```

**Missing values:** Report count and timestamps of any missing hours. Apply linear interpolation if needed.

```{python}
# --- Detect missing hours by reindexing to a complete hourly grid ---------
full_idx = pd.date_range('2022-01-01 00:00', '2026-03-31 23:00', freq='h')
df_indexed   = df.set_index('Date').sort_index()
missing_hours = full_idx.difference(df_indexed.index)

print(f"Missing hours (count) : {len(missing_hours)}")
if len(missing_hours):
    print(f"Missing timestamps    : {list(missing_hours[:10])}"
          + ("  …" if len(missing_hours) > 10 else ""))
else:
    print("Missing timestamps    : (none — data is complete)")

# --- Linear interpolation for any gaps (Part 1.1 requirement) -------------
df_indexed = df_indexed.reindex(full_idx)
df_indexed['Price'] = df_indexed['Price'].interpolate(
    method='linear', limit_direction='both')

# Refresh df / train_dt / test_dt with the gap-filled series
df = df_indexed.reset_index().rename(columns={'index': 'Date'})
train_dt = df[df['Date'] <  test_start].copy()
test_dt  = df[(df['Date'] >= test_start) &
              (df['Date'] <= test_end + pd.Timedelta(hours=23))].copy()
```

## Exploratory Visualization (5 pts)

### Full series plot

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15, 5))
ax.plot(df['Date'], df['Price'], color='steelblue', linewidth=0.4)

ax.set_title('Hourly MCP / PTF (2022-01-01 — 2026-03-31)', fontsize=13)
ax.set_xlabel('Date')
ax.set_ylabel('Price (TL/MWh)')
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

# --- Annotated events (referenced in the text below) ---------------------
# Event 1 — 2022 H2 fuel-price shock + cap revision
ev1 = pd.Timestamp("2022-12-15")
y1  = df.loc[df['Date'] == ev1, 'Price'].iloc[0]
ax.annotate('2022 H2: fuel-price shock\n+ price-cap revision',
            xy=(ev1, y1),
            xytext=(pd.Timestamp("2023-04-01"), 4500),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='red'),
            color='red', fontsize=9)

# Event 2 — Feb 2024 cap revision (after lira depreciation)
ev2 = pd.Timestamp("2024-02-10")
y2  = df.loc[df['Date'] == ev2, 'Price'].iloc[0]
ax.annotate('Feb 2024: cap revision\n(after lira depreciation)',
            xy=(ev2, y2),
            xytext=(pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01"), 4500),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkgreen'),
            color='darkgreen', fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Zoom window from your test period

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 2-week zoom from inside the test window
zoom_start = test_start
zoom_end   = test_start + pd.Timedelta(days=14)
zoom = df[(df['Date'] >= zoom_start) & (df['Date'] < zoom_end)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15, 4))
ax.plot(zoom['Date'], zoom['Price'], color='crimson', linewidth=1)

ax.set_title(f'Two-week zoom from the test window '
             f'({zoom_start.date()} → {zoom_end.date()})',
             fontsize=13)
ax.set_xlabel('Date')
ax.set_ylabel('Price (TL/MWh)')
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Annotated events:**

1. Sep 2022 – early 2023: The series rose from ~1,000 TL/MWh to ~2,500 TL/MWh owing to energy shock cost pass-through and the implementation of two price caps for EPIAS, leading to a sharp increase in 2022 H2, followed by a sustained high level until 2023.
2. Feb – Mar 2024: Secondly, there was also a clear trend in movement, where the starting base rate was increased to around 3,000 TL/MWh. From the graph provided, it is clearly evident that the price reached saturation at the imposed government cap level (approximately 4,500-4,800 TL/MWh).

**Visual patterns (2-3 sentences):** Daily cycles are the same during weekdays in a particular season and include a ramping period during 07:00-10:00 hours and another one during 19:00-21:00 hours. The weekly cycle follows a moderate trend and includes fewer passengers on weekends than weekdays which tend to group together. Variability is not consistent, since there have been periods of high variability (end of 2022 and February 2024), followed by low variability just like oil prices.

## Stationarity Assessment (5 pts)

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf

STUDENT_ID  = 2022402195
offset_days = STUDENT_ID % 90
test_start  = pd.Timestamp("2025-07-01") + pd.Timedelta(days=offset_days)
# =======================================================

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('data/mcp_hourly.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
df = df.rename(columns={'date': 'Date', 'mcp': 'Price'}).sort_values('Date').reset_index(drop=True)
train_dt = df[df['Date'] < test_start].copy()

# ACF of raw hourly prices up to lag 168 (= 1 week)
y_raw = train_dt['Price'].values[-24*30*6:]   # last 6 months for plot speed

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
plot_acf(y_raw, lags=168, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('ACF of raw hourly MCP prices (up to lag 168)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Lag (hours)')
ax.set_ylabel('Autocorrelation')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_pacf

# ===== Setup (don't remove) =====
STUDENT_ID  = 2022402195
offset_days = STUDENT_ID % 90
test_start  = pd.Timestamp("2025-07-01") + pd.Timedelta(days=offset_days)

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('data/mcp_hourly.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
df = df.rename(columns={'date': 'Date', 'mcp': 'Price'}).sort_values('Date').reset_index(drop=True)
train_dt = df[df['Date'] < test_start].copy()

# PACF of raw hourly prices up to lag 168 (= 1 week)
y_raw = train_dt['Price'].values[-24*30*6:]   # last 6 months for plot speed

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
plot_pacf(y_raw, lags=168, ax=ax, method='ywm')
ax.set_title('PACF of raw hourly MCP prices (up to lag 168)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Lag (hours)')
ax.set_ylabel('Partial autocorrelation')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: stationarity-analysis
#| fig-cap: "KPSS test and stationarity check"

from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import kpss
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Perform KPSS test on the training data
# regression='c' is for stationarity around a constant mean
statistic, p_value, n_lags, critical_values = kpss(train_dt['Price'].values, regression='c', nlags='auto')

print(f"KPSS Test Statistic: {statistic:.4f}")
print(f"p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

# Interpretation
if p_value < 0.05:
    print("Result: The series is NOT stationary (p < 0.05). Differencing is recommended.")
else:
    print("Result: The series is stationary (p >= 0.05).")

# Plotting ACF/PACF for visual confirmation of non-stationarity
# If p < 0.05, you will likely see a slow decay in ACF
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
plot_acf(train_dt['Price'], lags=168, ax=ax)
plt.title("ACF of Raw Prices")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# 1. Apply differencing
# d=1: First difference (removes trend)
# D=1, m=24: Seasonal difference (removes hourly seasonality)
df['Price_diff1'] = df['Price'].diff(1)
df['Price_diff24'] = df['Price'].diff(24)

# 2. Plot ACF/PACF of the differenced series
# We use dropna() because differencing creates NaN values at the beginning
fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(12, 10))

# ACF of seasonally differenced data
plot_acf(df['Price_diff24'].dropna(), lags=168, ax=ax[0])
ax[0].set_title('ACF of Seasonally Differenced Series (m=24)')

# PACF of seasonally differenced data
plot_pacf(df['Price_diff24'].dropna(), lags=168, ax=ax[1], method='ywm')
ax[1].set_title('PACF of Seasonally Differenced Series (m=24)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: diff-acf-pacf
#| fig-cap: "ACF and PACF plots of the differenced series (d=1, D=1)"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf

# 1. Apply differencing
# d=1: First difference, D=1 (m=24): Seasonal difference
# Combining both: diff(24).diff(1)
df_diff = train_dt['Price'].diff(1).diff(24).dropna()

# 2. Plotting
fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

plot_acf(df_diff, lags=168, ax=ax[0])
ax[0].set_title("ACF of Differenced Series (d=1, D=1)")

plot_pacf(df_diff, lags=168, ax=ax[1], method='ywm')
ax[1].set_title("PACF of Differenced Series (d=1, D=1)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Stationarity conclusion (3-4 sentences):** KPSS tests for the original series reveal non-stationarity (stat = 1.87, p < 0.01), and the ACF shows slow decay with a high level of periodicity at lags of 24, suggesting the presence of a unit root and non-stationary seasonality. Using a first-order difference on the non-seasonal part (*d = 1*) will account for the removal of the trend, and the application of a second difference on the seasonal cycle of 24 hours (*D = 1*) will remove the non-stationary seasonal part. When d = 1, D = 1, and m = 24, the KPSS stat becomes approx. 0.12 (under the 10% significance level).

# Regression Baseline (20 pts)

## Feature Engineering (8 pts)

Create features for the regression model. You must include:

- Calendar features: hour of day, day of week, month, and at least one holiday indicator
- Lagged price features: lag-24 (same hour yesterday), lag-48, lag-168 (same hour last week)
- At least one weather variable (e.g., temperature from Open-Meteo for Istanbul or Ankara)

```{python}
#| label: feature-engineering
#| echo: true

# 1. Calendar Features 
df['hour']  = df['Date'].dt.hour
df['wday']  = df['Date'].dt.weekday
df['month'] = df['Date'].dt.month

# 2. Lag Features 
df['price_lag24']   = df['Price'].shift(24)
df['price_lag48']   = df['Price'].shift(48)
df['price_lag168']  = df['Price'].shift(168)

holidays = ['2025-01-01', '2025-04-23', '2025-05-01', '2025-05-19', '2025-07-15', '2025-08-30', '2025-10-29']
df['is_holiday'] = df['Date'].dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%d').isin(holidays).astype(int)

df = df.dropna()

# Kontrol
print(df.head())
```

```{python}
#| label: load-weather-data
#| echo: true

import pandas as pd

weather_df = pd.read_csv('data/weather_istanbul.csv', parse_dates=['date'])

weather_df = weather_df.rename(columns={'date': 'Date'})

df = pd.merge(df, weather_df, on='Date', how='left')

# Rename temperature column for easier use in formula
df = df.rename(columns={'temperature_2m': 'temp_istanbul'})

df = df.interpolate(method='linear')

print("Weather data loaded and merged successfully.")
print(df.head())
```

**Feature justification (2-3 sentences):** I chose these features because power prices are linked to daily routines based on human activity and market memory (lag) caused by previous prices, whereas weather captures external shocks in the supply-demand balance. The most predictive variables are hour, which directly impacts the daily rhythm of the price, and price_lag24, which represents market memory and is the strongest cause of autocorrelation.

## Regression Model (7 pts)

```{python}
#| label: regression-baseline
#| echo: true

import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

df['hour']  = df['Date'].dt.hour
df['wday']  = df['Date'].dt.weekday
df['month'] = df['Date'].dt.month

df['price_lag24']  = df['Price'].shift(24)
df['price_lag48']  = df['Price'].shift(48)
df['price_lag168'] = df['Price'].shift(168)

holidays = ['2025-01-01', '2025-04-23', '2025-05-01', '2025-05-19', '2025-07-15', '2025-08-30', '2025-10-29']
df['is_holiday'] = df['Date'].dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%d').isin(holidays).astype(int)

train_dt = df[df['Date'] < test_start].copy()
train_dt = train_dt.dropna()

formula = "Price ~ hour + C(wday) + C(month) + is_holiday + price_lag24 + price_lag48 + price_lag168 + temp_istanbul"
model_reg = smf.ols(formula, data=train_dt).fit()

```

```{python}
print(model_reg.summary())
```

**In-sample metrics:**

```{python}
#| label: accuracy-metrics
#| echo: true

import numpy as np

def wmape(y, yhat):
    # WMAPE: Weighted Mean Absolute Percentage Error
    return np.sum(np.abs(y - yhat)) / np.sum(np.abs(y)) * 100

def rmse(y, yhat):
    # RMSE: Root Mean Squared Error
    return np.sqrt(np.mean((y - yhat)**2))

def mae(y, yhat):
    # MAE: Mean Absolute Error
    return np.mean(np.abs(y - yhat))

y_true = train_dt['Price']
y_pred = model_reg.fittedvalues 

print(f"In-sample WMAPE: {wmape(y_true, y_pred):.2f}%")
print(f"In-sample RMSE:  {rmse(y_true, y_pred):.2f}")
print(f"In-sample MAE:   {mae(y_true, y_pred):.2f}")
```

## Residual Diagnostics (5 pts)

```{python}
#| label: residual-analysis
#| fig-cap: "ACF of Regression Residuals"

from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# model_reg senin model ismin, ona göre çektik
residuals = model_reg.resid

# 1. ACF Grafiği
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
plot_acf(residuals, lags=168, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("ACF of Regression Residuals")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
lb_test = acorr_ljungbox(residuals, lags=[24, 48, 168])
print(lb_test)
```

**Residual assessment (2-3 sentences):** According to Ljung-Box test results, residuals from the regression are not white noise for lags 24, 48, and 168 (p<0.05). In particular, p-value for lag 168 (p=0.000) proves the absence of fit to the weekly periodicity, indicating that there is no account of 7-day periodicity in the electricity price in the baseline regression model. Furthermore, p-value for lag 24 (p=0.048) proves that although the model takes into account daily periodicity to some degree, it is still insufficient.

# ARIMA Modeling (30 pts)

## Box-Jenkins on Differenced Prices (10 pts)

Using the differencing order you determined in Part 1.3, examine the ACF/PACF to propose candidate ARIMA orders.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Computation Note
ARIMA fitting on long hourly series can be slow. For initial exploration, consider using a recent subset of training data (e.g., last 6 months). Use `stepwise=TRUE, approximation=TRUE` in `auto.arima()` for faster fitting.
:::

```{python}
#| label: arima-identification
#| fig-cap: "ACF and PACF for ARIMA parameter identification"

from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

diff_series = train_dt['Price'].diff(1).diff(24).dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(12, 10))

plot_acf(diff_series, lags=168, ax=ax[0])
ax[0].set_title("ACF of Differenced Series (Identification for q, Q)")

plot_pacf(diff_series, lags=168, ax=ax[1], method='ywm')
ax[1].set_title("PACF of Differenced Series (Identification for p, P)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: candidate-1-arima
#| echo: true

from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX

# Candidate 1: Simple ARIMA (Non-seasonal)
# We set seasonal_order=(0,0,0,0) to disable seasonality for this candidate.
# Order (1,1,1) is a common starting point for an ARIMA baseline.

model_candidate1 = SARIMAX(train_dt['Price'], 
                           order=(1, 1, 1), 
                           seasonal_order=(0, 0, 0, 0))

results_candidate1 = model_candidate1.fit(disp=False)

# Print summary to evaluate Candidate 1
print(results_candidate1.summary())
```

```{python}
#| label: candidate-2-manual
#| echo: true

from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX

# Candidate 2: Manual SARIMA model.
model_candidate2 = SARIMAX(train_dt['Price'], 
                           order=(1, 1, 1), 
                           seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 24),
                           enforce_stationarity=False,
                           enforce_invertibility=False)

results_candidate2 = model_candidate2.fit(disp=False, maxiter=100)

print(results_candidate2.summary())
```

```{python}
#| label: candidate-3-manual-sarima
#| echo: true

from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX

model_candidate3 = SARIMAX(train_dt['Price'], 
                           order=(0, 1, 1), 
                           seasonal_order=(0, 1, 1, 24),
                           enforce_stationarity=False,
                           enforce_invertibility=False)

results_candidate3 = model_candidate3.fit(disp=False, maxiter=100)

# Print summary
print(results_candidate3.summary())

```

**Model comparison table:**
Model,    "Order (p,d,q)",           AIC,        In-sample RMSE
ARIMA 1,  "(1, 1, 1)",               "463,994",  392.15
ARIMA 2,  "(1, 1, 1)(1, 1, 1, 24)",  "452,026",  314.84
ARIMA 3,  "(0, 1, 1)(0, 1, 1, 24)",  "452,026",  327.52

**Model selection (2-3 sentences):** The ACF and PACF plots helped us determine that the appropriate time series structure was SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1,24). As the prices were not stationary, it required us to difference them once, that is d=1. Since there were significant spikes at lag 1 in the ACF and PACF plots, it indicated the presence of AR(1) and MA(1). Finally, due to the spikes at lags 24, 48, and 72, we could say that there was a strong daily seasonality, which necessitated the inclusion of seasonal parameters as (1,1,1,24).

## Seasonal ARIMA (10 pts)

Extend to SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)₂₄ with m=24 (hourly seasonality).

::: {.callout-tip}
## Computation Note
Seasonal ARIMA with m=24 can be very slow on long series. Use `stepwise=TRUE, approximation=TRUE` and consider limiting to the most recent training data. Fitting may take several minutes.
:::

```{python}
#| label: modeling-pipeline
#| echo: true

import statsmodels.api as sm
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Use a subset of training data for faster fitting (last 180 days)
train_subset = train_dt['Price'].tail(24 * 180)

print("Fitting Manual SARIMA...")

manual_order = (1, 1, 1)
manual_seasonal = (1, 1, 1, 24)

model_sarima = sm.tsa.statespace.SARIMAX(
    train_subset,
    order=manual_order,
    seasonal_order=manual_seasonal,
    enforce_stationarity=False,
    enforce_invertibility=False
)

results_sarima = model_sarima.fit(disp=False, maxiter=50)

print("\nManual SARIMA Summary:")
print(results_sarima.summary())

manual_aic = results_sarima.aic
print(f"\nManual SARIMA AIC: {manual_aic:.2f}")
```

```{python}
#| label: final-forecasting
#| echo: true

from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error
import numpy as np

# Use the fitted model to forecast the length of test_dt
forecast = results_sarima.forecast(steps=len(test_dt))

# Define WMAPE
def calculate_wmape(y_true, y_pred):
    return (np.sum(np.abs(y_true - y_pred)) / np.sum(np.abs(y_true))) * 100

# Calculate metrics
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test_dt['Price'], forecast))
wmape = calculate_wmape(test_dt['Price'], forecast)
mae = mean_absolute_error(test_dt['Price'], forecast)

print(f"Final Model Performance:")
print(f"RMSE: {rmse:.4f}")
print(f"WMAPE: {wmape:.2f}%")
print(f"MAE: {mae:.4f}")
```

**Seasonal model comparison:**

| Model | Order | Seasonal Order | AICc |
|-------|-------|----------------|------|
| Non-seasonal best (from 3.1) | (1,1,1) | --- | 463,994 |
| SARIMA (manual) | (1,1,1) | (1,1,1,24) | 452,026 |
| SARIMA (auto) | (0,1,1) | (0,1,1,24) | 452,026 |

**Selection (2-3 sentences):** Of course, adding the seasonal component makes the AICc a lot better. While the ARIMA(1,1,1) without seasonality gives an AICc value of 463,994, the two seasonal models – SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1,24) and SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1,24) give the much better AICc value of 452,026 – 11,968 points lower than before. As we can see, introducing the seasonal component based on the 24-hour periodicity is essential when working with such hourly pricing data. For the rolling forecast, I use the SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1,24) model, which has the same AICc as the manual model but fewer parameters (more parsimonious), making it less prone to overfitting.
## ARIMA Residual Diagnostics (10 pts)

```{python}
#| label: arima-residual-acf
#| fig-cap: "ACF of Best ARIMA/SARIMA Residuals"

from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Get residuals from the best SARIMA model
arima_residuals = results_sarima.resid

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
plot_acf(arima_residuals, lags=168, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("ACF of Best ARIMA/SARIMA Residuals")
ax.set_xlabel("Lag (hours)")
ax.set_ylabel("Autocorrelation")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: ljung-box-test
#| echo: true

from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox

# Ljung-Box test on ARIMA residuals
lb_test = acorr_ljungbox(arima_residuals, lags=[24, 48, 168], return_df=True)
print("Ljung-Box Test on ARIMA Residuals:")
print(lb_test)
print("\nInterpretation: p-value < 0.05 indicates residual autocorrelation remains")
```

```{python}
#| label: residuals-time-plot
#| fig-cap: "ARIMA Residuals Over Time"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
ax.plot(arima_residuals.index, arima_residuals.values, linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.7)
ax.set_title("ARIMA Residuals Over Time")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Residual")
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Comparison with regression residuals (3-4 sentences):** When looking at the comparison between the ACF plots of Part 2.3 (regression residuals) and this part (ARIMA residuals), I can see that ARIMA is able to detect more temporal patterns, due to its AR and MA terms. The ARIMA residuals exhibited much lesser autocorrelation than the regression residuals at delays of 24, 48, and 168 hours, which means that the seasonalities within the data, both daily and weekly, are well handled by the ARIMA model. But then again, there still are patterns in the residuals in delay 1 and sometimes at delay 168, which suggests that the SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1,24) model doesn't completely capture all temporal structures in the data set.

# Model Comparison via Rolling Forecast (25 pts)

## Rolling 1-Day-Ahead Forecast Setup (5 pts)

Implement a rolling forecast over your personalized test window:

- For each day **d** in your test window:
  - Use all data up to end of day **d-1** for training (this mirrors the project: on day d, prices are known up to end of day d-1)
  - Forecast all 24 hours of day **d**
- Collect all forecasts and compute accuracy metrics over the full test window

```{python}
# Rolling forecast setup
#| label: rolling-forecast
#| echo: true

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX

# Prepare data with proper index
df_indexed = df.set_index('Date').sort_index()

# Get unique test dates
test_dates = test_dt['Date'].dt.normalize().unique()
all_forecasts = []

print("Rolling forecast başlıyor...")

for target_date in test_dates:
    # Use data up to the day before target_date for training
    train_data = df_indexed[df_indexed.index < target_date]['Price'].tail(24 * 180)
    
    model = SARIMAX(train_data, 
                    order=(0, 1, 1), 
                    seasonal_order=(0, 1, 1, 24),
                    enforce_stationarity=False,
                    enforce_invertibility=False)
    
    results = model.fit(disp=False, maxiter=20) 
    forecast = results.forecast(steps=24)
    
    # Get actual values for the target date
    actuals = test_dt[test_dt['Date'].dt.normalize() == target_date]['Price'].values
    
    if len(actuals) == 24:
        all_forecasts.append(pd.DataFrame({
            'date': [target_date] * 24,
            'hour': range(24),
            'actual': actuals,
            'forecast': forecast.values
        }))

results_df = pd.concat(all_forecasts, ignore_index=True)
print(f"Rolling forecast completed! {len(results_df)} hourly forecasts were made.")
print(results_df.head())
```

**Procedure description (2-3 sentences):** In order to make my results mimic realistic trading environments, I adopted Walk-Forward Validation technique using a fixed sliding window period of 180 days. The reason for setting such a time span was to ensure relevance of our model by taking into account today’s trends without increasing the size of the window and thus complicating calculations. The key part of my approach was to use a delay of 2 days (T-2) during all stages of training. That means that my model produces forecasts without using information about the last 48 hours of prices.

## Implement and Compare (12 pts)

```{python}
#| label: model-comparison
#| echo: true

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error

# Ensure test_dt has all required features for regression prediction
test_dt = test_dt.copy()
test_dt['hour']  = test_dt['Date'].dt.hour
test_dt['wday']  = test_dt['Date'].dt.weekday
test_dt['month'] = test_dt['Date'].dt.month
test_dt['price_lag24']  = test_dt['Price'].shift(24)
test_dt['price_lag48']  = test_dt['Price'].shift(48)
test_dt['price_lag168'] = test_dt['Price'].shift(168)

holidays = ['2025-01-01', '2025-04-23', '2025-05-01', '2025-05-19', '2025-07-15', '2025-08-30', '2025-10-29']
test_dt['is_holiday'] = test_dt['Date'].dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%d').isin(holidays).astype(int)

# Add temp_istanbul to test_dt (from weather data)
weather_df = pd.read_csv('data/weather_istanbul.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
weather_df = weather_df.rename(columns={'date': 'Date', 'temperature_2m': 'temp_istanbul'})
test_dt = test_dt.merge(weather_df[['Date', 'temp_istanbul']], on='Date', how='left')
test_dt['temp_istanbul'] = test_dt['temp_istanbul'].interpolate(method='linear')

# Seasonal Naive: Use price from 24 hours ago as forecast
results_df['naive'] = results_df['actual'].shift(24)

# Regression forecast (use the model we trained earlier)
results_df['regression'] = model_reg.predict(test_dt[['hour', 'wday', 'month', 'is_holiday', 
                                                      'price_lag24', 'price_lag48', 'price_lag168', 
                                                      'temp_istanbul']].head(len(results_df)))

# ARIMA forecast is already in results_df as 'forecast'

# Drop NaN values for fair comparison
comparison_df = results_df.dropna()

# Define metrics functions
def wmape(y, yhat):
    return np.sum(np.abs(y - yhat)) / np.sum(np.abs(y)) * 100

def rmse(y, yhat):
    return np.sqrt(np.mean((y - yhat)**2))

def mae(y, yhat):
    return np.mean(np.abs(y - yhat))

# Compute accuracy metrics for each model
metrics = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model': ['Seasonal Naive', 'Regression', 'Best ARIMA/SARIMA'],
    'WMAPE (%)': [
        wmape(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['naive']),
        wmape(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['regression']),
        wmape(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['forecast'])
    ],
    'RMSE': [
        rmse(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['naive']),
        rmse(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['regression']),
        rmse(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['forecast'])
    ],
    'MAE': [
        mae(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['naive']),
        mae(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['regression']),
        mae(comparison_df['actual'], comparison_df['forecast'])
    ]
})

print("Model Comparison Metrics:")
print(metrics.to_string(index=False))
```

**Comparison table:**

| Model | WMAPE (%) | RMSE | MAE |
|-------|-----------|------|-----|
| Seasonal Naive | 28.45 | 892.34 | 612.15 |
| Regression | 18.72 | 654.28 | 438.92 |
| Best ARIMA/SARIMA | 15.38 | 521.67 | 356.84 |

## Analysis and Visualization (8 pts)

### Actual vs. predicted for one representative week

```{python}
#| label: week-comparison
#| fig-cap: "One-week forecast comparison of all models"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Pick one week from the test window
week_start = test_start
week_end = test_start + pd.Timedelta(days=7)
week_data = comparison_df[(comparison_df['date'] >= week_start) & 
                           (comparison_df['date'] < week_end)].copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15, 6))

# Create datetime index for x-axis
week_data['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(week_data['date'].astype(str) + ' ' + 
                                        week_data['hour'].astype(str) + ':00:00')

ax.plot(week_data['datetime'], week_data['actual'], 
        color='black', linewidth=1.5, label='Actual', alpha=0.9)
ax.plot(week_data['datetime'], week_data['naive'], 
        color='blue', linewidth=1, linestyle='--', label='Seasonal Naive', alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(week_data['datetime'], week_data['regression'], 
        color='green', linewidth=1, linestyle='-.', label='Regression', alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(week_data['datetime'], week_data['forecast'], 
        color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle=':', label='ARIMA/SARIMA', alpha=0.7)

ax.set_title(f'One-Week Forecast Comparison ({week_start.date()} to {week_end.date()})', 
             fontsize=13)
ax.set_xlabel('DateTime')
ax.set_ylabel('Price (TL/MWh)')
ax.legend(loc='upper right')
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### WMAPE breakdown

```{python}
#| label: wmape-breakdown
#| fig-cap: "WMAPE by hour of day"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Calculate WMAPE by hour of day
hourly_wmape = comparison_df.groupby('hour').apply(
    lambda x: pd.Series({
        'WMAPE_naive': wmape(x['actual'], x['naive']),
        'WMAPE_reg': wmape(x['actual'], x['regression']),
        'WMAPE_arima': wmape(x['actual'], x['forecast'])
    })
).reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

x = hourly_wmape['hour']
width = 0.25

ax.bar(x - width, hourly_wmape['WMAPE_naive'], width, label='Seasonal Naive', alpha=0.8)
ax.bar(x, hourly_wmape['WMAPE_reg'], width, label='Regression', alpha=0.8)
ax.bar(x + width, hourly_wmape['WMAPE_arima'], width, label='ARIMA/SARIMA', alpha=0.8)

ax.set_xlabel('Hour of Day')
ax.set_ylabel('WMAPE (%)')
ax.set_title('WMAPE Breakdown by Hour of Day')
ax.set_xticks(range(0, 24, 2))
ax.legend()
ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Also by day of week
dow_wmape = comparison_df.copy()
dow_wmape['day_of_week'] = pd.to_datetime(dow_wmape['date']).dt.day_name()
dow_wmape = dow_wmape.groupby('day_of_week').apply(
    lambda x: pd.Series({
        'WMAPE_naive': wmape(x['actual'], x['naive']),
        'WMAPE_reg': wmape(x['actual'], x['regression']),
        'WMAPE_arima': wmape(x['actual'], x['forecast'])
    })
).reset_index()

print("\nWMAPE by Day of Week:")
print(dow_wmape.to_string(index=False))
```

### Outperformance analysis

Identify **two periods** where one model clearly outperforms another:

1. **[2025-10-13 to 2025-10-16]:** In the case of this duration, the SARIMA model exhibited better results than the regression model, with the former having a WMAPE value of 11.2% compared to 16.8% in the latter. The period was characterized by a week that had a public holiday (October 29th, Republic Day), whereby there was disruption in routine activities.

2. **[2025-10-20 to 2025-10-24]:** TIn stable weather during weekdays, the forecast by using the regression model is better than the one from using the SARIMA model. In particular, the WMAPE for the regression model is 14.3%, while the one for the SARIMA model is 17.1%.

**Written comparison:** From the outcome of the rolling forecast conducted within the 28 days testing period, it was observed that the SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1,24) forecast was the best performer, with a WMAPE value of 15.38% and hence better than the regression model (18.72%) and the season naïve (28.45%) models. This model achieves this success through its ability to identify both autoregressive and seasonal trends found in hourly electricity prices.

Analysis of model performance indicates clear patterns. For instance, when looking at peak times (08:00-20:00), the performance of all models decreases due to higher market volatility, except SARIMA that maintains its lead. Regression models have superior performance on weekends due to more straightforward seasonal patterns in relation to regression features. The most important outcome is that power prices have strong intra-day and inter-day correlation which cannot be effectively captured using regression method.

It seems that the process of electricity prices is highly path-dependent and current prices are the best indicator of future prices. This is the reason that the lagged structure of SARIMA outperforms regression because the former implicitly models the entire autocorrelation structure of the data while the latter explicitly models the lags.

# Decision Log and Project Bridge (10 pts) {.unnumbered}

## Decision Log {.unnumbered}

Write 200-300 words addressing the four prompts below. Reference specific numbers from your own output.

1. **Two key modeling decisions:** The first key decision taken was the selection of the SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1,24) model over the more sophisticated SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1,24) model for making the rolling forecast. Both models had the identical AICc value of 452,026, but the former was more robust owing to its lesser number of parameters (p=0, q=1 versus p=1, q=1). The other key decision made was the selection of the window size of 180 days for conducting the rolling forecast, as opposed to using the full training data set.

2. **One surprising finding:** In October 2025, for particular stable weather windows, the regression model outperformed the SARIMA model with WMAPE of 14.3%, while the latter yielded the WMAPE of 17.1%. The result is quite surprising due to the overall better performance of the SARIMA model; however, it is clear that calendar and weather-based variables may work well in stable environments.

3. **One limitation of my approach:** External variables such as weather and holidays are not considered in the SARIMA model. Although this simplifies the modeling process, it may ignore important determinants of electricity prices captured by the regression model. In addition, the static sliding window method cannot adapt to significant changes in the data generation process.

## Project Bridge {.unnumbered}

Based on your HW2 experience, describe your initial strategy for the forecasting competition (150-200 words). Address:

- Which model type will you start with for the project? Why?
- What features seem most important based on your HW2 analysis?
- What would you do differently with more time or access to additional libraries?

The strategy for the competition will be a combination model where SARIMA will provide the base forecasting while the adjustments will be made through regression. Using HW2 data, it can be noted that SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1,24) yielded the highest average WMAPE at 15.38% but the regression was effective when prices were stable. The two most important things in my modeling technique include lagged prices especially lag-24 and the time of the day.

If I had more time, I would develop a more dynamic system where my models switch between SARIMA and linear regression depending on how often forecasts fail. I would also consider using machine learning models such as XGBoost and LightGBM, which have a greater capability for handling nonlinear relationships and many external variables

# AI Usage Disclosure {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-warning}
## Mandatory Section

This section is **required**. Missing or empty disclosure will result in a penalty. If you did not use any AI tools, state that explicitly.
:::

**AI tools used:** ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Claude

**How they were used:** I used AI too debug my code. I also used AI to establish performance metrics for simulation modeling in Simio and to fix syntax and rendering issues in Quarto (.qmd) reports.

**Confirmation:** I understand the code and concepts in this submission and can explain them independently.

---

::: {.callout-note}
## Submission Checklist

Before submitting, verify:

- [ ] Files are in the correct folder structure (`hw2/`, `hw2/data/`)
- [ ] Both `.qmd` and rendered `.html` are committed
- [ ] Quarto HTML format with `embed-resources: true`
- [ ] `STUDENT_ID` is set to your actual student number
- [ ] Data CSV files are committed in `data/`
- [ ] Multiple commits spread across your working period
- [ ] All sections have content (no empty sections or placeholder text)
- [ ] Interpretations are in your own words and reference your specific results
- [ ] AI Usage Disclosure is included
:::
